speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]. Tape number three. Freddy Diament, Auschwitz III, Monowitz Buna, April 19, 1996.

speaker: interviewee
text: Finally, I would say a few words about the wonderful Jewish men who were in the Buna camp. I made so many friends there. They were such wonderful, intelligent people that I admired, that have taught me. After all, I was only a teenager. I grew up in Buna, practically. It behooves us who survived not to forget them. To remember them, and tell the world that, even in the greatest inferno, in the greatest-- in the worst hell, human beings can have-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] --character and a spirit, so to speak. If they have the right spirit, they will triumph over every evil. And that took place in Buna. All the dehumanization efforts of the Nazis really did not succeed in that camp. And primarily because we had a few hundred wonderful, wonderful leaders that, by their example, encouraged us all and inspired us all. And it is our obligation, those that were lucky enough to survive to always to remember them and to tell the world what was going on here so that never, never again will something like this happen to anybody. And I would like to introduce a very good friend of mine who was also with me together in Buna. My friend, Sigi Hart, who also was there at the same time, when the three, among them my-- my-- my wonderful brother, were hanged. So here's Sigi Hart, and why don't you say a little bit what you have to say about Buna.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were together in the camp. And I remember very well the execute-- the murder of--

speaker: interviewee
text: Leo.

speaker: interviewee
text: --Freddy's brother in October. And-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] --we were working in the-- the famous [NON-ENGLISH], the-- [? FD: ?] Complex. --prisoner. The prisoners built this complex. [BACKGROUND NOISE] From A to Z, it was built up by prisoners from all-- from all the nations. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We had some youngsters there also, from Greek-- from Greece-- who--

speaker: interviewee
text: Salonika.

speaker: interviewee
text: --from Saloniki, who tried to survive. And-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] --they were promised a little piece of bread if they could fight, if they could give a show to the SS as fighters.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah, yeah. It just came to my mind. It was impossible to save thousands. And here the underground came in again. We knew the greatest chance, if anybody had a chance to survive, was youngsters, people that were 16, 17, 15, 18 years old. And if we had a few-- a-- a-- a-- a-- a few occasions to help those youngsters by arranging that they get easy jobs.

speaker: interviewee
text: For example, I myself worked for some time for the Lager-- for the camp elder. And in his name, I went every day to the kitchen and told him that the Lager-- the camp elder commandeered them to give me an extra barrel of soup. And I remember like yesterday, the-- the Greek-- the-- the Greek were concentrated in tents. You remember that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: There were big tents. They treated them like animals. There were 1,500 people like sardines on the floor. There were no bunks. Were sleeping there like sardines. And those young kids were so hungry. Every night I had 50, 60 kids coming, and they got a-- a-- a little of soup, and that saved their lives. Sigi was also involved in this. He also helped those Greek. And we-- both of us, when we went to Israel and they saw us, they come and hugged us and told us right in our face, you saved our lives. And-- and seeing those kids going up to grownup mans, and have families and children, you know, it gives you such great satisfaction that, even in the worst of circumstances, you stayed a human being. That's what it means to be a human being, to-- to-- to help your fellow man. Now it's your turn, Sigi.

speaker: interviewee
text: I just want to mention what is a-- a destiny that we were both together in the camps. We went through all this hardship. We went both to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: Israel, then Palestine. We both fought in the army, in the '48 and the '56 war. And then we got kind of separated. He was a kibbutznik. He went to the kibbutz.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to the city, and the next time I heard from him was in Los Angeles. All of a sudden I hear Freddy Diament is in Los Angeles. And of course we connected together again, and how closer can you get. I was on-- on his children's bar mitz-- bris and bar mitzvahs and weddings, and the same with him. He was on mine. And on top of it, his sons-- his grandson and my grandson go together in the same school. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: The same school.

speaker: interviewee
text: Today.

speaker: interviewee
text: Let me say this. Those bonds from the camps are literally stronger than real blood-based family bonds. We have so much in common. We think the same way. We have the same philosophy of life. And-- and that friendship can never be destroyed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, even in business. You remember? Isn't that amazing? I was only in business, working for a big company, Sigi-- again, can you imagine? Sigi started his own business. And by sheer coincidence, we saw each other, downtown Los Angeles, and he-- and he said to me, I just started a business. We need work.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and he filled me up, the place with work over the head. I mean--

speaker: interviewee
text: I put him in business. I mean--

speaker: interviewee
text: We helped each other in the camps, and we help each other in life.

speaker: interviewee
text: Without taking any credit, it is so-- if he were to [NON-ENGLISH], it is so--

speaker: interviewee
text: Natural.

speaker: interviewee
text: Natural, without any question asked. And that is really human relations and true friendship. I think so.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is true friendship.

speaker: interviewee
text: And now we're here today. Can you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we-- we made this trip. I-- I'm here after 51 years the first time, and-- and we came here to-- to honor our-- our friends--

speaker: interviewee
text: In honor of friends.

speaker: interviewee
text: And loved ones who are not with us anymore. To honor them.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.
